1: A theme of Christological conflict: the Pharisees and scribes who have come from Jerusalem gather around Jesus — the religious authorities of the capital now enter the Galilean ministry to interrogate. The gathering of Jerusalem officials at this point signals that the conflict has escalated beyond local disputes; the confrontation that will end at the cross has its theological roots in these controversies over authority, Torah interpretation, and purity.
2: A theme of ritual purity and the new covenant: the disciples eat with “common” (koinais — ritually unwashed) hands. The purity system Jesus is about to reinterpret was not peripheral to Jewish identity — it marked the boundary between Israel and the nations. Christ's response will redefine that boundary from external ritual to internal character, opening the covenant community to all who have clean hearts rather than clean hands.
3: A revelation of God's purpose through Christ: the parenthetical explanation of Jewish hand-washing customs shows Mark writing for a Gentile audience unfamiliar with these practices. Mark's explanatory aside signals that the teaching Jesus is about to give (Mark 7:14–23) is meant precisely for Gentile readers — the declaration that all foods are clean is already addressed to the church that will include them.
4: A theme of tradition confronting Christ: the Pharisees observe many additional purification requirements for markets, cups, pots, and bronze vessels. These accretions to the written Torah represent the “tradition of the elders” (Mark 7:3) — the oral law that the Pharisees treated as binding. Christ will distinguish between the commandment of God and the tradition of men (Mark 7:8), and the distinction will redefine the entire purity framework.
5: A theme of Christological challenge: the Pharisees and scribes ask why the disciples do not walk according to the tradition of the elders. The question is really about authority — whose interpretation of Torah governs? Christ's answer claims an authority over Torah interpretation that transcends the scribal tradition, positioning himself as the one who speaks God's commandment rather than expounding it.
6: A direct citation fulfilled: “Isaiah was right when he prophesied about you hypocrites: this people honors me with their lips but their heart is far from me.” The citation is Isaiah 29:13, which Isaiah applied to a form of religion that had substituted human performance for genuine covenant loyalty. Christ applies it to the Pharisees, positioning himself as the one in whose ministry the prophetic critique finds its final referent — and who will inaugurate the new covenant written on hearts (Jeremiah 31:33), not performed with hands.
7: A direct citation: “in vain do they worship me, teaching as doctrines the commandments of men.” The second half of the Isaiah citation identifies the specific failure: human traditions elevated to divine status. Christ as the Word of God (John 1:1) is the authoritative divine commandment — his teaching does not supplement the tradition of the elders but supersedes it as the genuine voice of God that the tradition claimed to preserve.
8: A revelation of Christ's authority over Torah: “You leave the commandment of God and hold to the tradition of men.” Christ distinguishes the commandment of God (which he upholds and fulfills) from the tradition of men (which he sets aside). This distinction is foundational to his teaching ministry — he does not abolish the Torah but fulfills it (Matthew 5:17), cutting through the interpretive overlay to reveal the original divine intent that the oral tradition had obscured.
9: A revelation of Christological authority: Jesus says “You have a fine way of rejecting the commandment of God in order to establish your tradition.” The irony is sharp — the keepers of Torah have inverted its priorities. Christ speaks as the one who knows the commandment's original intent because he is the Word through whom it was given. His interpretive authority is not a teacher's superior scholarship but the Author's knowledge of his own text.
10: A direct citation: “Moses said, Honor your father and your mother, and Whoever reviles father or mother must surely die.” Christ cites the fifth commandment (Exodus 20:12; Deuteronomy 5:16) and its capital sanction (Exodus 21:17; Leviticus 20:9) as the standard being violated by the corban practice. His appeal to Moses' commandment against the oral law's corban extension shows him as the champion of Torah's original intent against its corruption — a role he fulfills definitively at the cross where he honors the Father perfectly.
11: A revelation of Christ exposing religious exploitation: the corban (qorban) formula allowed a person to declare property dedicated to God, thereby exempting it from parental support while retaining personal use. Christ identifies this as a mechanism that uses the language of devotion to God to avoid the commandment of God. The same critique applies wherever religious performance is used as cover for disobeying God's moral demands — a pattern Christ addresses throughout his ministry.
12: A theme of Christ and Torah's priority: “you no longer permit him to do anything for his father or mother.” The oral tradition's corban ruling created a binding legal outcome that contradicted the written Torah. Christ's correction restores the commandment's priority — and the principle he establishes (God's written commandment over human interpretive tradition) is the hermeneutic that governs his own interpretive method throughout Mark.
13: A revelation of Christ's understanding of tradition's corruption: “making void the word of God by your tradition that you have handed on. And many such things you do.” The word of God (logos tou theou) that human tradition nullifies is the commandment Christ upholds. The contrast between the word of God and human tradition is the contrast between Christ's teaching and the scribal method — he speaks what God originally said; they speak what men have added to it.
14: A revelation of Christ calling all to hear: Jesus calls the crowd to him and says, “Hear me, all of you, and understand.” The summons to universal hearing (pantas — everyone) before the clean/unclean declaration is Christological: he is about to speak a principle that will open the covenant to all peoples. The call to “hear and understand” echoes the Shema structure — the hearing that Israel was commanded to do is now directed toward Christ's word.
15: A direct revelation: “Nothing outside a person can defile them by going in; rather, it is what comes out that defiles.” This is the central Christological principle of the purity controversy and one of Jesus's most radical teachings. The entire Levitical food-law system was predicated on external contact producing impurity; Christ locates defilement in the moral life of the heart. The new covenant promise of a clean heart (Ezekiel 36:26) is being enacted: Christ speaks the principle that the Spirit will fulfill in his people.
16: A revelation of Christ's call to spiritual hearing: “If anyone has ears to hear, let him hear.” The formula is used at moments of special revelation — it marks what has just been said as a teaching that requires spiritual perception, not merely intellectual processing. The inability of the disciples to understand it (Mark 7:18) shows that the new-covenant ear — the circumcised heart — is not yet universally present. That transformation will come through Christ's Spirit after Pentecost.
17: A theme of progressive revelation: in the house, away from the crowd, the disciples ask Jesus about the parable. The pattern of public teaching followed by private explanation to the inner circle runs throughout Mark (Mark 4:10–20, Mark 9:28, Mark 10:10) — the house is the space where deeper disclosure happens. The disciples' need for private explanation reflects their incomplete understanding, which Mark presents as the normal pre-resurrection condition that the Spirit will rectify.
18: A revelation of Christ's patience with slow understanding: “Are you also without understanding?” The disciples' inability to grasp the clean/unclean principle — even after private explanation — is the kind of dullness Mark documents throughout the pre-resurrection narrative. Christ is patient with incomplete perception while consistently pressing toward full understanding. The disciples who fail to understand now will be the ones who preach the Gentile mission later, once the Spirit has opened their ears.
19: A direct revelation: the parenthetical “In saying this, he declared all foods clean” is Mark's interpretive comment on the Christological implications of Mark 7:15. The declaration that defiling comes from within, not without, has as its direct corollary the abolition of the Levitical food distinctions. This is a new-covenant act: Christ as the divine Lawgiver declares the purity code fulfilled in himself and no longer binding. Acts 10:15 (“What God has made clean, do not call common”) is the Spirit confirming what Christ declared here.
20: A theme of the heart's corruption addressed by Christ: “What comes out of a person is what defiles him.” The teaching locates the locus of corruption in the human heart — the same diagnosis given in Genesis 6:5 (“every intention of the heart was only evil”) and Jeremiah 17:9 (“the heart is deceitful”). Christ comes to address that root, not merely manage its symptoms. The new covenant promise (Jeremiah 31:31–34; Ezekiel 36:26) is the divine response to this diagnosis, and Christ is its mediator.
21: A revelation of Christ diagnosing the human condition: the catalog of evils that proceed from the heart — evil thoughts, sexual immorality, theft, murder, adultery — is a Christological statement about why the incarnation was necessary. These are not peripheral failures requiring correction but symptoms of a fundamental disorder of the will that only the one who can give a new heart (Ezekiel 36:26) can remedy. Christ names what he has come to heal.
22: A theme of the inner corruption Christ overcomes: coveting, wickedness, deceit, sensuality, envy, slander, pride, foolishness — the second half of the catalog. These are the works of the flesh (Galatians 5:19–21) that Christ's atonement addresses at the root. The purpose of naming them is not condemnation but diagnosis — Christ is the physician (Mark 2:17) who knows the disease perfectly because he has come to bear it and cure it.
23: A direct revelation: “All these evil things come from within and defile a person.” The conclusion of the purity discourse reframes the entire Levitical cleansing system: the defilement it was pointing at is moral and spiritual, not ceremonial. The law's external cleanness provisions were pedagogical — they taught the concept of holiness and the necessity of God's cleansing. Christ fulfills that pedagogy and delivers the reality: the Spirit-wrought inner cleanness (Titus 3:5) that the washings symbolized.
24: A revelation of Christ breaking geographic barriers: Jesus departs to the region of Tyre and Sidon — Gentile territory, outside the covenant land. The movement is deliberate and anticipates the Gentile mission: the one who declared all foods clean now crosses the boundary into Gentile space. His attempted privacy (he did not want anyone to know) is overcome by the need of a Gentile woman — as the privacy of his early Galilean ministry was repeatedly overcome by need.
25: A revelation of Christ's reputation crossing ethnic lines: a Syrophoenician woman hears of him and comes to fall at his feet. The woman is Greek (Hellēnis) and Syrophoenician by birth — a double Gentile identity. The posture of falling at his feet is the posture of worship and supplication that only the truly great can evoke. Christ is being approached with the kind of desperate faith that Mark has already shown produces miracles — the faith of the four men (Mark 2:4), the woman with bleeding (Mark 5:27), Jairus (Mark 5:22).
26: A theme of persistent faith: the woman begs Jesus to cast the demon out of her daughter. The request is simple and urgent — no theological sophistication, no membership in Israel, no legal claim on the covenant. She comes with need and faith alone. The response Jesus gives (Mark 7:27) tests that faith rather than immediately granting it — and she passes the test, demonstrating the quality of faith that receives from Christ regardless of ethnic status.
27: A direct revelation of covenant priority and expansion: “Let the children be fed first, for it is not right to take the children's bread and throw it to the dogs.” The “first” (prōton) is the key word — Paul will use it in Romans 1:16 (“to the Jew first and also to the Greek”). Jesus is not denying Gentile access but stating the covenantal sequence: Israel is the vessel of the promise, the one through whom the Messiah comes, and through whom blessing flows to the nations (Genesis 12:3). The children eat first; the others then eat.
28: A direct revelation through the woman's response: “Lord, even the dogs under the table eat the children's crumbs.” The woman accepts the covenantal priority but claims the overflow — the Gentile's portion is not the primary inheritance but the abundance that spills from it. Jesus grants the request immediately: her answer is the answer of faith that grasps how the covenant works. The crumbs from the children's table are sufficient for her daughter's healing — a prefiguration of Gentile salvation through Israel's Messiah.
29: A direct revelation: “For this statement you may go your way; the demon has left your daughter.” The healing word is spoken at a distance — Christ does not go to the girl, does not touch her, does not require her presence. The word alone is sufficient across Gentile geographic space. This is the only Markan healing granted on the basis of the petitioner's argument rather than physical contact or presence — a Christological statement that his authority is not spatially limited by the covenant boundaries.
30: A theme of Christ's authority over the demonic in Gentile space: the woman goes home and finds the child lying on the bed with the demon gone. The result is the same as every exorcism in Jewish territory — complete, immediate, confirmed. Christ's authority over demonic powers is universal, not ethnically bounded. The Gentile child receives the same liberation as those in Capernaum. The mission that will be commanded after the resurrection (Mark 16:15) is already being enacted in miniature.
31: A theme of Christ's movement through Gentile regions: Jesus returns from Tyre through Sidon to the Sea of Galilee via the Decapolis — a route through largely Gentile territory. The geographic circuit is significant: the declaration that all foods are clean (Mark 7:19) is followed by extended presence in Gentile lands. The theological shift produces the geographic shift; the one who has opened the covenant boundary moves through the space beyond it.
32: A revelation of Christ as healer: people bring a deaf-mute man and beg Jesus to lay his hand on him. The deaf and mute condition is both physical and symbolic — the inability to hear and speak is the condition of the spiritually deaf and mute whom Christ's kingdom comes to restore (Isaiah 35:5–6). The begging community intercedes for one who cannot speak for himself; the healing Christ is approached through others' faith on behalf of the one in need.
33: A revelation of Christ's compassion in method: Jesus takes the man aside privately, puts his fingers in his ears, spits, and touches his tongue. The unusual physical gestures (unique in the Gospels to this healing and the two-stage blind man healing in Mark 8:22–26) communicate the healing intention through action to one who cannot hear the word. Christ accommodates himself to the man's condition — he meets the deaf man in the language of touch and gesture, not speech.
34: A direct revelation: looking up to heaven, Jesus sighs and says “Ephphatha” — “Be opened.” The upward look signals the filial relationship (the Son acting in dependence on the Father); the sigh (stenazō) signals genuine compassionate engagement with human brokenness. “Be opened” is the language of creation (God who opens the ears of the deaf in Isaiah 35:5) spoken by the Son in his own name. The Aramaic is preserved — one of Mark's instances of the original healing word retained as a mark of authentic testimony.
35: A direct fulfillment of Isaiah: immediately his ears were opened and his tongue was released and he spoke plainly. Isaiah 35:5–6 prophesied that when God returned to save his people, the deaf would hear and the mute would sing. The deaf-mute restored to full hearing and speech is the fulfillment of that prophecy — the messianic age has arrived in Jesus's person and ministry. The word “plainly” (orthos) suggests not just speech but correct, clear speech — the full restoration of the communication faculty.
36: A theme of the Messianic Secret: Jesus commands them to tell no one, but the more he charges them, the more zealously they proclaim it. The irony structures the Markan secrecy motif: the identity that cannot be contained cannot be silenced. The one who commands silence produces proclamation. After the resurrection, what was commanded in silence will be commanded in proclamation (Mark 16:15); the secret of the cross becomes the gospel of the resurrection.
37: A direct revelation through acclamation: “He has done all things well; he even makes the deaf hear and the mute speak.” The echo of Genesis 1:31 (“God saw all that he had made, and it was very good”) is deliberate — the crowd uses the Creator's language of assessment for what Jesus has done. The new creation has begun: the one whose word was “good” at the first creation speaks the word that restores broken creation to its original goodness. This is the healing that only God can perform (Psalms 94:9).
